narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which actually stands above the typical 3.21 average among past students—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from similar students. The model estimated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk level that tipped the decision toward rejection to prioritize applicants with stronger predicted odds of passing. Among the key factors influencing this outcome, your undergraduate GPA emerged as the most significant positive force, pushing the prediction toward success because it signals stronger academic preparation than most. However, your LSAT score of 31, which falls below the average of 36.7, played a notable role in countering that by nudging the outlook toward potential failure, as lower scores in this area have historically correlated with bar struggles for students like you. Your middle family income bracket also contributed toward the failure prediction to a meaningful degree, reflecting patterns where this socioeconomic level has sometimes aligned with added challenges in bar preparation compared to higher brackets. Your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 4—below averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—along with your full-time student status, which is common for over 90% of applicants, were less decisive but fit into the overall picture. In essence, while your GPA gives you a real edge over many peers, the LSAT and income factors pulled enough weight to create uncertainty about bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background pursuing full-time studies—it's a data-driven call based on how similar profiles have fared. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening any recent academic deciles through additional coursework or highlighting bar prep experience in reapplications might also help. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so persistence could turn this into an opportunity for a stronger application next time.
